intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

body.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

draft.title: recipe app for people who hate cooking
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
recipe app for people who hate cooking

Building your first draft…

data.intent: recipe app for people who hate cooking

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer low-visual-noise, dark mode designs that feel effortless and less overwhelming
facade.label: Minimal dark interface
facade.acceptImplies: Build with dark palette, sparse layouts, and high whitespace to reduce cognitive load
facade.rejectImplies: Users may want warmth, color, or visual playfulness rather than austere minimalism

evidence.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking prefer low-visual-noise, dark mode designs that feel effortless and less overwhelming
evidence.implication: Build with dark palette, sparse layouts, and high whitespace to reduce cognitive load

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to an encouraging, celebratory tone than a neutral instructional one
facade.axisTargeted: Mood and atmosphere — energetic vs calm
facade.label: Cheerful, motivating voice
facade.acceptImplies: The app should use upbeat language, celebratory moments, and positive reinforcement throughout the UX (e.g., 'You crushed it!' vs 'Recipe complete')
facade.rejectImplies: The app should adopt a quieter, more matter-of-fact tone that doesn't oversell the cooking process or assume emotional investment

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking respond better to a supportive, motivational tone that celebrates small wins and makes the process feel achievable rather than intimidating
facade.label: Warm & encouraging
facade.acceptImplies: Lean into emotional reassurance, celebratory language, and a mentor-like voice that meets users where they are (overwhelmed, skeptical, time-poor)
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward efficiency-focused, no-nonsense, matter-of-fact language that prioritizes speed and clarity over emotional support

facade.hypothesis: Breaking recipe instructions into a single scrollable column with each step numbered and compact reduces cognitive load for people who find cooking intimidating
facade.axisTargeted: Dense vertical scroll vs. paginated steps
facade.label: Inline recipe steps
facade.acceptImplies: User prefers linear, continuous discovery of instructions; likely values speed and reduced friction over segmented decision points
facade.rejectImplies: User may prefer bite-sized, isolated steps (one per screen/page); suggests tolerance or preference for paced, deliberate progression through cooking process

facade.hypothesis: Users expect recipes to auto-populate a shopping list as they browse, reducing friction in the planning phase
facade.axisTargeted: Gesture vs click — automatic behavior vs manual action
facade.label: Ingredient auto-fills
facade.acceptImplies: Design should favor seamless, background automation and anticipatory UX patterns throughout the app
facade.rejectImplies: Design should favor explicit user control and deliberate clicks — users want to confirm every choice rather than have the app assume intent

axes.label: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
Whether user wants a calm, low-pressure environment vs. gamified, achievement-driven experience with rewards
Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. ingredient sourcing and substitution options
axes.poleA: Speed and skill level or ingredient swaps

axes.label: Whether user wants instant recipe access vs. animated transitions and progressive reveals that slow down the experience

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants visual simplicity and minimal ingredient lists vs. rich food photography and detailed plating inspiration
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers step-by-step guided workflows vs. ingredient-first browsing and flexible navigation
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to encouraging, motivational tone vs. irreverent, humorous tone that validates cooking aversion
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants a calm, low-pressure environment vs. gamified, achievement-driven experience with rewards
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prioritizes time estimates and difficulty ratings vs. ingredient sourcing and substitution options
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant recipe access vs. animated transitions and progressive reveals that slow down the experience
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: People who hate cooking prefer step-by-step task lists (with checkboxes) over narrative recipe prose—because it reduces cognitive load and creates visible progress.
facade.axisTargeted: Dense data vs key metrics — or more precisely: procedural clarity (checkbox-driven sequential steps) vs narrative flow (traditional recipe storytelling)
facade.label: Recipe as checklist
facade.acceptImplies: Show recipes as scannable, progressively-revealable checklists with ingredient checkoffs, timer triggers, and completion states. Minimize decorative prose. Embrace task-completion feedback.
facade.rejectImplies: Users may prefer either: (a) ingredient + macro info *cards* (visual chunking over prose), or (b) conversational voice that makes cooking feel less like a *chore* and more like guidance—suggesting the anti-pattern is over-gamification/task language for this audience.

facade.hypothesis: Users who hate cooking might respond better to visual warmth and vibrant color—photos of fresh, appealing ingredients—rather than pure minimalism, as it makes the process feel less clinical and more inviting
facade.axisTargeted: Color temperature and saturation (warm/vibrant vs. cool/muted)
facade.label: Bright colorful ingredients
facade.acceptImplies: Designs should use bright, appetizing photography and color to reduce the friction of cooking by making it feel more joyful and less like a chore
facade.rejectImplies: Users strongly prefer restrained, neutral palettes—confirming that visual restraint (not visual warmth) is key to the 'hate cooking' audience

draft.title: QuickBite – Recipe App for People Who Hate Cooking
draft.summary: A recipe app that strips away complexity and delivers dead-simple meals in under 15 minutes with minimal ingredients and zero fuss.
draft.html:
QuickBite

Meals so easy, you'll actually cook them

Filter by mood
Today's picks
Pasta aglio e olio
8 min
4 ingredients No skill needed
Avocado toast
3 min
3 ingredients Zero cooking
Scrambled eggs
5 min
2 ingredients Foolproof
